name: Vishnu Avatars
shortDescription: The Avatars of Vishnu are the দশ avataras (incarnations) assumed by the Hindu god Vishnu to preserve dharma and restore cosmic balance on Earth.
description: In Hindu mythology, the Avatars of Vishnu (also known as Dashavatara when referring to the ten principal avatars) are the incarnations assumed by the god Vishnu to descend to Earth and restore dharma (righteousness) whenever it is threatened by evil. Vishnu, as the preserver of the universe, takes on these forms to maintain cosmic balance and protect his devotees. The most commonly recognized are the ten Dashavatara: Matsya (fish), Kurma (tortoise), Varaha (boar), Narasimha (man-lion), Vamana (dwarf), Parashurama (axe-wielding Rama), Rama (the virtuous king), Krishna (the divine statesman), Buddha (the enlightened one), and Kalki (the future savior). Each avatar appears in a specific yuga (era) to combat specific forms of adharma and uphold the cosmic order. The concept of avatars underscores the cyclical nature of time and the continuous struggle between good and evil in the Hindu worldview. The stories of the avatars are rich with symbolism and moral lessons, offering guidance on righteous living and devotion. The avatars demonstrate Vishnu's immanence and transcendence, showing that the divine is both present in the world and beyond it. The diverse forms of the avatars also reflect the adaptability and multifaceted nature of the divine.
